title: My employer did not pay me for 2 months, what should I do?

messages.content: My employer did not pay me for 2 months, what should I do?

triage_result.Original Input: My employer did not pay me for 2 months, what should I do?
triage_result.Normalized English: The user is seeking legal guidance regarding the non-payment of wages by their employer for a period of two months.

metadata.title: . Time of payment. Wages shall be paid at least once every two (2) weeks or twice a month at
metadata.parent_context:
. Time of payment. Wages shall be paid at least once every two (2) weeks or twice a month at 
intervals not exceeding sixteen (16) days. If on account of force majeure or circumstances beyond the 
employer’s control, payment of wages on or within the time herein provided cannot be made, the employer 
shall pay the wages immediately after such force majeure or circumstances have ceased. No employer shall 
make payment with less frequency than once a month. 
The payment of wages of employees engaged to perform a task which cannot be completed in two (2) weeks 
shall be subject to the following conditions, in the absence of a collective bargaining agreement or arbitration 
award: 
1. That payments are made at intervals not exceeding sixteen (16) days, in proportion to the amount of 
work completed; 
   
2. That final settlement is made upon completion of the work.

rag_chunks.text:
. Time of payment. Wages shall be paid at least once every two (2) weeks or twice a month at 
intervals not exceeding sixteen (16) days. If on account of force majeure or circumstances beyond the 
employer’s control, payment of wages on or within the time herein provided cannot be made, the employer 
shall pay the wages immediately after such force majeure or circumstances have ceased. No employer shall 
make payment with less frequency than once a month. 
The payment of wages of employees engaged to perform a task which cannot be completed in two (2) weeks 
shall be subject to the following conditions, in the absence of a collective bargaining agreement or arbitration 
award: 
1. That payments are made at intervals not exceeding sixteen (16) days, in proportion to the amount of 
work completed; 
   
2. That final settlement is made upon completion of the work.

metadata.title: Calculation of average monthly wages for section 32NA
metadata.source_file: Cap 57 Consolidated version for the Whole Chapter (24-08-2025) (English).pdf
metadata.parent_context:
(1)	
This section applies in calculating an employee’s average 
monthly wages for the purposes of section 32NA(1)(b)(ii).
	
(2)	
In subsections (3), (4) and (5)—
wages (工資) includes a sum of money paid by an employer in 
respect of any of the following days—
	
(a)	
a day of maternity leave, a day of paternity leave, a rest 
day, a sickness day, a holiday or a day of annual leave 
taken by the employee;
	
(b)	
a day of leave taken by the employee with the agreement 
of the employer;
	
(c)	
a normal working day on which the employee is not 
provided with work by the employer;
	
(d)	
a day of absence from work of the employee due to 
temporary incapacity for which compensation is payable 
under section 10 of the Employees’ Compensation 
Ordinance (Cap. 282).
	
(3)	
The employee’s average monthly wages are the average 
monthly wages earned by the employee during—
	
(a)	
the period of 12 months immediately before the date of 
termination of the contract of employment; or
	
(b)	
if the employee has been employed by the employer for 
a period shorter than 12 months immediately before the 
date of termination of the contract of employment—the 
shorter period.
Last updated date
16.6.2023
Employment Ordinance
Part VIA	
6A-40
Section 32NB	
Cap. 57
Verified Copy
	
(4)	
The average monthly wages are to be calculated without 
regard to—
	
(a)	
any period (excluded period) during the 12-month 
period or shorter period for which the employee was not 
paid wages or full wages because of—
	
(i)	
any maternity leave, paternity leave, rest day, 
sickness day, holiday or annual leave taken by the 
employee;
	
(ii)	
any leave taken by the employee with the 
agreement of the employer;
	
(iii)	
the employee’s not being provided with work by 
the employer on a normal working day; or
	
(iv)	
the employee’s absence from work due to 
temporary incapacity for which compensation 
is payable under section 10 of the Employees’ 
Compensation Ordinance (Cap. 282); and
	
(b)	
any wages paid to the employee for the excluded period.
	
(5)	
To avoid doubt, if the amount of the wages paid to an 
employee in respect of a day covered by the definition of 
wages in subsection (2) is only a fraction of the amount 
earned by the employee on a normal working day, the 
employee’s average monthly wages are to be calculated 
without regard to the wages and the day.
	
(6)	
Despite subsection (3), if for any reason it is impracticable to 
calculate an employee’s average monthly wages in the manner 
provided in that subsection, the amount may be calculated by 
reference to—
	
(a)	
the wages earned by a person who was employed at the 
same work by the same employer during the period of 
12 months immediately before the date of termination of 
the employee’s contract of employment; or
Last updated date
16.6.2023
Employment Ordinance
Part VIA	
6A-42
Section 32NB	
Cap. 57
Verified Copy
	
(b)	
if there is no such person—the wages earned by a person 
who was employed in the same trade or occupation and 
at the same work in the same district during the period 
of 12 months immediately before the date of termination 
of the employee’s contract of employment.
(Added 21 of 2018 s. 5)

metadata.title: Employer to supply information at request of employee
metadata.source_file: Cap 57 Consolidated version for the Whole Chapter (24-08-2025) (English).pdf
metadata.parent_context:
(1)	
Where an employer who is a sub-contractor fails to pay, 
within the period specified in section 23, 24 or 25, as the case 
may be, any wages due to an employee employed by him on 
work which he has contracted to perform, he shall within 7 
days of the receipt of a written request made by the employee 
supply to the employee the name and address of the principal 
contractor and every superior sub-contractor to him and shall, 
within such 7 days’ period, deliver a copy of the written 
request to the principal contractor and every superior sub-
contractor to him.
	
(2)	
An employer who without reasonable excuse fails to comply 
with subsection (1) shall be guilty of an offence and shall be 
liable on conviction to a fine at level 5. (Amended 24 of 1988 
s. 2; 103 of 1995 s. 15)

metadata.title: Rate of paternity leave pay
metadata.source_file: Cap 57 Consolidated version for the Whole Chapter (24-08-2025) (English).pdf
metadata.parent_context:
(1)	
In this section—
specified date (指明日期), in relation to paternity leave taken by 
an employee, means—
	
(a)	
if the leave is taken in a period of consecutive days, the 
date on which that period begins; or
	
(b)	
in any other case, the date on which the leave is taken;
wages (工資), in subsections (2), (3) and (4), includes a sum of 
money paid by an employer in respect of any of the following 
days—
	
(a)	
a day of paternity leave, a rest day, a sickness day, a 
holiday or a day of annual leave taken by the employee;
	
(b)	
a day of leave taken by the employee with the agreement 
of the employer;
	
(c)	
a normal working day on which the employee is not 
provided with work by the employer;
	
(d)	
a day of absence from work of the employee due to 
temporary incapacity for which compensation is payable 
under section 10 of the Employees’ Compensation 
Ordinance (Cap. 282).
	
(2)	
The daily rate of paternity leave pay is four-fifths of the 
employee’s average daily wages during—
Last updated date
11.12.2020
Employment Ordinance
Part IIIA	
3A-8
Section 15I	
Cap. 57
Verified Copy
	
(a)	
the period of 12 months immediately before the 
specified date; or
	
(b)	
if the employee has been employed by the employer for 
a period shorter than 12 months immediately before the 
specified date, the shorter period.
	
(3)	
The average daily wages are to be calculated without regard 
to—
	
(a)	
any period (excluded period) during the 12-month 
period or shorter period for which the employee was not 
paid wages or full wages because of—
	
(i)	
any paternity leave, rest day, sickness day, holiday 
or annual leave taken by the employee;
	
(ii)	
any leave taken by the employee with the 
agreement of the employer;
	
(iii)	
the employee’s not being provided with work by 
the employer on a normal working day; or
	
(iv)	
the employee’s absence from work due to 
temporary incapacity for which compensation 
is payable under section 10 of the Employees’ 
Compensation Ordinance (Cap. 282); and
	
(b)	
any wages paid to the employee for the excluded period.
	
(4)	
To avoid doubt, if the amount of the wages paid to an 
employee in respect of a day covered by the definition of 
wages in subsection (1) is only a fraction of the amount 
earned by the employee on a normal working day, the 
employee’s average daily wages are to be calculated without 
regard to the wages and the day.
	
(5)	
Despite subsection (2), if for any reason it is impracticable to 
calculate an employee’s average daily wages in the manner 
provided in that subsection, the amount may be calculated by 
reference to—
Last updated date
11.12.2020
Employment Ordinance
Part IIIA	
3A-10
Section 15I	
Cap. 57
Verified Copy
	
(a)	
the wages earned by a person who was employed at the 
same work by the same employer during the period of 
12 months immediately before the specified date; or
	
(b)	
if there is no such person, the wages earned by a person 
who was employed in the same trade or occupation and 
at the same work in the same district during the period 
of 12 months immediately before the specified date.
	
(6)	
If, under the employee’s contract of employment or any 
other agreement or for any other reason, the employer pays a 
sum of money to the employee in respect of a day on which 
the employee takes paternity leave, the paternity leave pay 
payable to the employee in respect of the day under this Part 
is to be reduced by the sum.

metadata.title: Wage period
metadata.source_file: Cap 57 Consolidated version for the Whole Chapter (24-08-2025) (English).pdf
metadata.parent_context:
The wage period in respect of which wages are payable under 
a contract of employment shall, until the contrary is proved, be 
deemed to be 1 month.

rag_chunks.text:

a contract of employment shall, until the contrary is proved, be 
deemed to be 1 month.

messages.content:
<think>
Hmm, this is a clear case of a migrant worker in Hong Kong facing non-payment of wages. The user is likely stressed about this situation, so I need to acknowledge that upfront while staying professional. 

The retrieved context is quite detailed but focuses on specific sections of Hong Kong's Employment Ordinance about wage calculation methods and payment timelines. The most relevant part is the principle that wages should be paid at least once every two weeks, which directly applies here. 

I should structure this by first showing empathy, then clearly stating the legal principle in simple terms, followed by how it applies to this two-month unpaid wages scenario. The conclusion should summarize the seriousness of the violation without giving legal advice, and end with an open question to understand if the user needs further clarification about their specific situation.

The safety contacts are necessary here since unpaid wages for two months constitutes a serious labor violation that likely requires official intervention. I'll include the relevant Hong Kong and Philippine agencies but keep it concise.
</think>

**1. APPLICATION & EMPATHY**

I understand that not receiving your salary for two months is an incredibly stressful and difficult situation. This creates significant hardship, and it's important to address it clearly. Let's break down the situation based on the information you've provided.

The key fact is: You are a worker in **Hong Kong** who has not been paid wages by your employer for a period of **two months**.

**2. THE LAW (SIMPLIFIED)**

The core legal principle here is very straightforward. In Hong Kong, the **Employment Ordinance** establishes strict rules for wage payment. The law requires employers to pay wages **on time** and **in full**.

While the retrieved context includes detailed rules for calculating wages in specific situations (like leave), the most fundamental rule is about payment frequency. Although not explicitly repeated in the provided text, it is a cornerstone of the law that wages must be paid at least once every month. Failing to pay wages on the due date is a serious violation.

**3. SIMPLE ANALYSIS**

Applying this principle to your scenario:
*   The employer has a clear obligation to pay you for the work you have done.
*   Not paying wages for two months is a significant breach of this basic obligation. There is generally no acceptable excuse for such a prolonged delay, barring extreme, unforeseen circumstances (often called "force majeure").
*   This situation typically means your employer is in violation of Hong Kong labor law. The unpaid wages are considered a debt owed to you by the employer.

**4. CONCLUSION & FOLLOW-UP**

In summary, the non-payment of wages for two months is a serious matter that generally constitutes a breach of your employment rights under Hong Kong law. The employer has a fundamental duty to pay salaries promptly.

Given the severity of this situation, which involves prolonged non-payment of wages, here are the official contacts that can provide assistance and handle such disputes:

*   **Hong Kong Labour Department:** They handle complaints about wage non-payment. You can visit their office or call their 24-hour enquiry hotline.
*   **Philippine Overseas Labor Office (POLO) in Hong Kong:** As part of the Philippine Consulate, they assist overseas Filipino workers (OFWs) with labor-related cases.
*   **DMW (Department of Migrant Workers) / OWWA (Overseas Workers Welfare Administration):** You or your family in the Philippines may also coordinate with these agencies for support.

To better understand the context, can you tell me if you have already tried speaking to your employer or your recruitment agency about this delayed payment?
